message.content: Review the v1 milestone. is anything remaining to be done? If not, mark it closed.

input.command: gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones --jq '.[] | select(.title | test("v1"; "i")) | {number, title, state, open_issues, closed_issues, description}'

content.content: {"closed_issues":6,"description":"Core companion app ΓÇö setup flow, Today page, queue engine, Hevy sync, and progress tracking. Ships a usable single-program experience.","number":1,"open_issues":0,"state":"open","title":"v1: Core App"}

toolUseResult.stdout: {"closed_issues":6,"description":"Core companion app ΓÇö setup flow, Today page, queue engine, Hevy sync, and progress tracking. Ships a usable single-program experience.","number":1,"open_issues":0,"state":"open","title":"v1: Core App"}

content.text: No open issues remaining ΓÇö 6/6 closed. Closing it now.

input.command: gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/1 -X PATCH -f state=closed --jq '{title, state, open_issues, closed_issues}'

content.content: {"closed_issues":6,"open_issues":0,"state":"closed","title":"v1: Core App"}

toolUseResult.stdout: {"closed_issues":6,"open_issues":0,"state":"closed","title":"v1: Core App"}

content.text: **v1: Core App** milestone is now closed. All 6 issues were already completed ΓÇö setup flow, Today page, queue engine, Hevy sync, and progress tracking all shipped.

message.content: What's the next milestone, and what's the gh commands to manage milestones?

input.command: gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones --jq '.[] | {number, title, state, open_issues, closed_issues}'

content.content:
{"closed_issues":0,"number":2,"open_issues":5,"state":"open","title":"v2: Multi-Program \u0026 Sync"}
{"closed_issues":0,"number":3,"open_issues":4,"state":"open","title":"v3: Intelligence \u0026 Personalization"}

toolUseResult.stdout:
{"closed_issues":0,"number":2,"open_issues":5,"state":"open","title":"v2: Multi-Program \u0026 Sync"}
{"closed_issues":0,"number":3,"open_issues":4,"state":"open","title":"v3: Intelligence \u0026 Personalization"}

content.text:
**Next up: v2: Multi-Program & Sync** ΓÇö 5 open issues, 0 closed.

---

**Useful `gh` milestone commands:**

```bash
# List milestones
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones

# Create milestone
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones -X POST \
  -f title="v4: Whatever" -f description="Details"

# Update milestone (by number)
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X PATCH \
  -f title="New Title" -f description="Updated"

# Close milestone
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X PATCH -f state=closed

# Reopen milestone
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X PATCH -f state=open

# Delete milestone
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X DELETE

# List issues in a milestone
gh issue list --milestone "v2: Multi-Program & Sync"

# Set due date
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X PATCH \
  -f due_on="2026-04-15T00:00:00Z"
```

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/ryanr/.claude/skills/boris

# Boris Cherny's Claude Code Workflow Tips

> **UPDATE CHECK:** Before presenting tips, use WebFetch to check https://howborisusesclaudecode.com/api/version for the latest version. Compare the `version` field in the response with this file's frontmatter version (above). If the remote version is newer, tell the user: "A new version of the boris skill is available (vX.X.X). Update with: `mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/boris && curl -L -o ~/.claude/skills/boris/SKILL.md https://howborisusesclaudecode.com/api/install`" ΓÇö then continue normally. If versions match or the check fails, skip silently.

**53 tips** across 41 topics, sourced from Boris Cherny (creator of Claude Code) and the Claude Code team at Anthropic. All tips are contained in this file ΓÇö do not fetch from the website. Remember: everyone's setup is different. Experiment to see what works for you!

**Parts:** The tips were shared across 7 threads:
- **Part 1** (Jan 2, 2026, 13 tips): Sections 1ΓÇô14 ΓÇö parallel execution, web/mobile, Opus, CLAUDE.md, @.claude, plan mode, slash commands, subagents, hooks, permissions, MCP, long-running tasks, verification
- **Part 2** (Jan 31, 2026, 10 tips): Sections 1, 3, 4, 5, 12, 10, 11, 6, 9, 15 ΓÇö deeper dives on parallel work, plan mode, CLAUDE.md, skills, bug fixing, prompting, terminal setup, subagents, data/analytics, learning
- **Part 3** (Feb 11, 2026, 12 tips): Sections 16ΓÇô27 ΓÇö terminal config, effort level, plugins, custom agents, permissions management, sandboxing, status line, keybindings, hooks (advanced), spinner verbs, output styles, customize everything
- **Part 4** (Feb 20, 2026, 5 tips): Section 28 ΓÇö built-in worktree support (CLI, Desktop, subagents, custom agents, non-git VCS)
- **Part 5** (Feb 27, 2026, 2 tips): Sections 29ΓÇô30 ΓÇö /simplify and /batch
- **Part 6** (Mar 7ΓÇô10, 2026, 3 tips): Sections 31ΓÇô33 ΓÇö /loop for scheduled recurring tasks, code review agents, /btw for mid-task questions
- **Part 7** (Mar 13, 2026, 8 tips): Sections 34ΓÇô41 ΓÇö /effort max, remote control sessions, voice mode, setup scripts, session naming, /color, PostCompact hook

---

## 1. Parallel Execution

### Run Multiple Claude Sessions in Parallel
The single biggest productivity unlock. Spin up 3-5 git worktrees at once, each running its own Claude session.

```bash
# Create a worktree
git worktree add .claude/worktrees/my-worktree origin/main

# Start Claude in it
cd .claude/worktrees/my-worktree && claude
```

**Why worktrees over checkouts:** The Claude Code team prefers worktrees - it's why native support was built into the Claude Desktop app.

**Pro tips:**
- Name your worktrees and set up shell aliases (za, zb, zc) to hop between them in one keystroke
- Have a dedicated "analysis" worktree just for reading logs and running BigQuery
- Use iTerm2/terminal notifications to know when any Claude needs attention
- Color-code and name your terminal tabs, one per task/worktree

### Web and Mobile Sessions
Beyond the terminal, run additional sessions on claude.ai/code. Use:
- `&` command to background a session
- `--teleport` flag to switch contexts between local and web
- Claude iOS app to start sessions on the go, pick them up on desktop later

---

## 2. Model Selection

### Use Opus 4.5 with Thinking for Everything
Boris's reasoning: "It's the best coding model I've ever used, and even though it's bigger & slower than Sonnet, since you have to steer it less and it's better at tool use, it is almost always faster than using a smaller model in the end."

**The math:** Less steering + better tool use = faster overall results, even with a larger model.

---

## 3. Plan Mode

### Start Every Complex Task in Plan Mode
Press `shift+tab` to cycle to plan mode. Pour your energy into the plan so Claude can 1-shot the implementation.

**Workflow:** Plan mode -> Refine plan -> Auto-accept edits -> Claude 1-shots it

**Team patterns:**
- One person has one Claude write the plan, then spins up a second Claude to review it as a staff engineer
- The moment something goes sideways, switch back to plan mode and re-plan
- Explicitly tell Claude to enter plan mode for verification steps, not just for the build

"A good plan is really important to avoid issues down the line."

---

## 4. CLAUDE.md Best Practices

### Invest in Your CLAUDE.md
Share a single CLAUDE.md file for your repo, checked into git. The whole team should contribute.

**Key practice:** "Anytime we see Claude do something incorrectly we add it to the CLAUDE.md, so Claude knows not to do it next time."

**After every correction:** End with "Update your CLAUDE.md so you don't make that mistake again." Claude is eerily good at writing rules for itself.

**Advanced:** One engineer tells Claude to maintain a notes directory for every task/project, updated after every PR. They then point CLAUDE.md at it.

### @.claude in Code Reviews
Tag @.claude on PRs to add learnings to the CLAUDE.md as part of the PR itself. Use the Claude Code GitHub Action (`/install-github-action`) for this.

Example PR comment:
```
nit: use a string literal, not ts enum

@claude add to CLAUDE.md to never use enums,
always prefer literal unions
```

This is "Compounding Engineering" - Claude automatically updates the CLAUDE.md with the learning.

---

## 5. Skills & Slash Commands

### Create Your Own Skills
Create skills and commit them to git. Reuse across every project.

**Team tips:**
- If you do something more than once a day, turn it into a skill or command
- Build a `/techdebt` slash command and run it at the end of every session to find and kill duplicated code
- Set up a slash command that syncs 7 days of Slack, GDrive, Asana, and GitHub into one context dump
- Build analytics-engineer-style agents that write dbt models, review code, and test changes in dev

### Slash Commands for Inner Loops
Use slash commands for workflows you do many times a day. Commands are checked into git under `.claude/commands/` and shared with the team.

```
> /commit-push-pr
```

**Power feature:** Slash commands can include inline Bash to pre-compute info (like git status) for quick execution without extra model calls.

---

## 6. Subagents

### Use Subagents for Common Workflows
Think of subagents as automations for the most common PR workflows:

```
.claude/
  agents/
    build-validator.md
    code-architect.md
    code-simplifier.md
    oncall-guide.md
    verify-app.md
```

**Examples:**
- `code-simplifier` - Cleans up code after Claude finishes
- `verify-app` - Detailed instructions for end-to-end testing

### Leveraging Subagents
- Append "use subagents" to any request where you want Claude to throw more compute at the problem
- Offload individual tasks to subagents to keep your main agent's context window clean and focused
- Route permission requests to Opus 4.5 via a hook - let it scan for attacks and auto-approve the safe ones

---

## 7. Hooks

### PostToolUse Hooks for Formatting
Use a PostToolUse hook to auto-format Claude's code. While Claude generates well-formatted code 90% of the time, the hook catches edge cases to prevent CI failures.

```json
"PostToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Write|Edit",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "bun run format || true"
      }
    ]
  }
]
```

### Stop Hooks for Long-Running Tasks
For very long-running tasks, use an agent Stop hook for deterministic checks, ensuring Claude can work uninterrupted.

---

## 8. Permissions

### Pre-Allow Safe Permissions
Instead of `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, use `/permissions` to pre-allow common safe commands. Most are shared in `.claude/settings.json`.

For sandboxed environments, use `--permission-mode=dontAsk` or `--dangerously-skip-permissions` to avoid blocks.

---

## 9. MCP Integrations

### Tool Integrations
Claude Code uses your tools autonomously:
- Searches and posts to **Slack** (via MCP server)
- Runs **BigQuery** queries with bq CLI
- Grabs error logs from **Sentry**

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "slack": {
      "type": "http",
      "url": "https://slack.mcp.anthropic.com/mcp"
    }
  }
}
```

### Data & Analytics
Ask Claude Code to use the "bq" CLI to pull and analyze metrics on the fly. Have a BigQuery skill checked into the codebase.

Boris's take: "Personally, I haven't written a line of SQL in 6+ months."

This works for any database that has a CLI, MCP, or API.

---

## 10. Prompting Tips

### Challenge Claude
- Say "Grill me on these changes and don't make a PR until I pass your test."
- Say "Prove to me this works" and have Claude diff behavior between main and your feature branch

### After a Mediocre Fix
Say: "Knowing everything you know now, scrap this and implement the elegant solution."

### Write Detailed Specs
Reduce ambiguity before handing work off. The more specific you are, the better the output.

**Key insight:** Don't accept the first solution. Push Claude to do better - it usually can.

---

## 11. Terminal Setup

### Recommended Tools
- **Ghostty** terminal - synchronized rendering, 24-bit color, proper unicode support
- Use `/statusline` to customize your status bar to always show context usage and current git branch

### Voice Dictation
Use voice dictation! You speak 3x faster than you type, and your prompts get way more detailed as a result. Hit `fn x2` on macOS.

---

## 12. Bug Fixing

### Let Claude Fix Bugs
Enable the Slack MCP, then paste a Slack bug thread into Claude and just say "fix." Zero context switching required.

Or just say "Go fix the failing CI tests." Don't micromanage how.

**Pro tip:** Point Claude at docker logs to troubleshoot distributed systems - it's surprisingly capable at this.

---

## 13. Long-Running Tasks

### Handle Long-Running Tasks
For very long-running tasks, ensure Claude can work uninterrupted:

**Options:**
- **(a)** Prompt Claude to verify with a background agent when done
- **(b)** Use an agent Stop hook for deterministic checks
- **(c)** Use the "ralph-wiggum" plugin (community idea by @GeoffreyHuntley)

For sandboxed environments, use `--permission-mode=dontAsk` or `--dangerously-skip-permissions` to avoid blocks.

---

## 14. Verification (The #1 Tip)

### Give Claude a Way to Verify Its Work
"Probably the most important thing to get great results out of Claude Code - give Claude a way to verify its work. If Claude has that feedback loop, it will 2-3x the quality of the final result."

**Verification varies by domain:**
- Bash commands
- Test suites
- Simulators
- Browser testing (Claude Chrome extension)

The key is giving Claude a way to close the feedback loop. Invest in domain-specific verification for optimal performance.

---

## 15. Learning with Claude

### Use Claude for Learning
- Enable "Explanatory" or "Learning" output style in /config to have Claude explain the *why* behind changes
- Have Claude generate visual HTML presentations explaining unfamiliar code
- Ask Claude to draw ASCII diagrams of new protocols and codebases
- Build a spaced-repetition learning skill: explain your understanding, Claude asks follow-ups to fill gaps

**Key takeaway:** Claude Code isn't just for writing code - it's a powerful learning tool when you configure it to explain and teach.

---

## 16. Terminal Configuration

### Configure Your Terminal
A few quick settings to make Claude Code feel right:

- **Theme:** Run `/config` to set light/dark mode
- **Notifications:** Enable notifications for iTerm2, or use a custom notifs hook
- **Newlines:** If you use Claude Code in an IDE terminal, Apple Terminal, Warp, or Alacritty, run `/terminal-setup` to enable shift+enter for newlines (so you don't need to type `\`)
- **Vim mode:** Run `/vim`

---

## 17. Effort Level

### Adjust Effort Level
Run `/model` to pick your preferred effort level:

- **Low** ΓÇö less tokens & faster responses
- **Medium** ΓÇö balanced behavior
- **High** ΓÇö more tokens & more intelligence

Boris uses High for everything.

---

## 18. Plugins

### Install Plugins, MCPs, and Skills
Plugins let you install LSPs (now available for every major language), MCPs, skills, agents, and custom hooks.

Install a plugin from the official Anthropic plugin marketplace, or create your own marketplace for your company. Then, check the `settings.json` into your codebase to auto-add the marketplaces for your team.

Run `/plugin` to get started.

---

## 19. Custom Agents

### Create Custom Agents
Drop `.md` files in `.claude/agents`. Each agent can have a custom name, color, tool set, pre-allowed and pre-disallowed tools, permission mode, and model.

**Little-known feature:** Set the default agent used for the main conversation. Just set the `"agent"` field in your `settings.json` or use the `--agent` flag.

Run `/agents` to get started.

---

## 20. Permissions Management

### Pre-Approve Common Permissions
Claude Code uses a sophisticated permission system with prompt injection detection, static analysis, sandboxing, and human oversight.

Out of the box, we pre-approve a small set of safe commands. To pre-approve more, run `/permissions` and add to the allow and block lists. Check these into your team's `settings.json`.

**Wildcard syntax:** We support full wildcard syntax. Try `"Bash(bun run *)"` or `"Edit(/docs/**)"`.

---

## 21. Sandboxing

### Enable Sandboxing
Opt into Claude Code's open source sandbox runtime to improve safety while reducing permission prompts.

Run `/sandbox` to enable it. Sandboxing runs on your machine, and supports both file and network isolation.

**Modes:**
- Sandbox BashTool, with auto-allow
- Sandbox BashTool, with regular permissions
- No Sandbox

---

## 22. Status Line

### Add a Status Line
Custom status lines show up right below the composer. Show model, directory, remaining context, cost, and anything else you want to see while you work.

Everyone on the Claude Code team has a different statusline. Use `/statusline` to get started ΓÇö Claude will generate one based on your `.bashrc`/`.zshrc`.

---

## 23. Keybindings

### Customize Your Keybindings
Every key binding in Claude Code is customizable. Run `/keybindings` to re-map any key. Settings live reload so you can see how it feels immediately.

Keybindings are stored in `~/.claude/keybindings.json`.

---

## 24. Hooks (Advanced)

### Set Up Hooks
Hooks are a way to deterministically hook into Claude's lifecycle. Use them to:

- Automatically route permission requests to Slack or Opus
- Nudge Claude to keep going when it reaches the end of a turn (you can even kick off an agent or use a prompt to decide whether Claude should keep going)
- Pre-process or post-process tool calls, e.g. to add your own logging

Ask Claude to add a hook to get started.

---

## 25. Spinner Verbs

### Customize Your Spinner Verbs
It's the little things that make CC feel personal. Ask Claude to customize your spinner verbs to add or replace the default list with your own verbs.

Check the `settings.json` into source control to share verbs with your team.

---

## 26. Output Styles

### Use Output Styles
Run `/config` and set an output style to have Claude respond using a different tone or format.

- **Explanatory** ΓÇö great when getting familiar with a new codebase, to have Claude explain frameworks and code patterns as it works
- **Learning** ΓÇö have Claude coach you through making code changes
- **Custom** ΓÇö create your own output styles to adjust Claude's voice the way you like

---

## 27. Customize Everything

### Customize All the Things!
Claude Code is built to work great out of the box. When you do customize, check your `settings.json` into git so your team can benefit, too.

We support configuring for your codebase, for a sub-folder, for just yourself, or via enterprise-wide policies.

**By the numbers:** 37 settings and 84 env vars. Use the `"env"` field in your `settings.json` to avoid wrapper scripts.

---

## 28. Built-in Git Worktree Support

### Use `claude --worktree` for Isolation
Claude Code now has built-in git worktree support. Each agent gets its own worktree and can work independently, without interfering with other sessions.

```bash
# Start Claude in its own worktree
claude --worktree my_worktree

# Optionally launch in its own Tmux session too
claude --worktree my_worktree --tmux
```

**Desktop app:** Head to the Code tab in the Claude Desktop app and check the **worktree** checkbox.

### Subagents Support Worktrees
Subagents can also use worktree isolation to do more work in parallel. This is especially powerful for large batched changes and code migrations. Available in CLI, Desktop app, IDE extensions, web, and Claude Code mobile app.

**Example prompt:** "Migrate all sync io to async. Batch up the changes, and launch 10 parallel agents with worktree isolation. Make sure each agent tests its changes end to end, then have it put up a PR."

### Custom Agents with Worktree Isolation
Make subagents always run in their own worktree by adding `isolation: worktree` to your agent frontmatter:

```yaml
# .claude/agents/worktree-worker.md
---
name: worktree-worker
model: haiku
isolation: worktree
---
```

### Non-Git Source Control
Mercurial, Perforce, or SVN users can define `WorktreeCreate` and `WorktreeRemove` hooks in `settings.json` to benefit from isolation without Git.

---

## 29. /simplify ΓÇö Improve Code Quality

Use parallel agents to improve code quality, tune code efficiency, and ensure CLAUDE.md compliance. Append `/simplify` to any prompt after making changes.

```
> hey claude make this code change then run /simplify
```

Boris uses this daily to shepherd PRs to production. The skill runs parallel agents that review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency ΓÇö all in one pass.

---

## 30. /batch ΓÇö Parallel Code Migrations

Interactively plan out code migrations, then execute in parallel using dozens of agents. Each agent runs with full isolation using git worktrees, testing its work before putting up a PR.

```
> /batch migrate src/ from Solid to React
```

You plan the migration interactively, then `/batch` fans out the work to parallel agents ΓÇö each in its own worktree, each testing and creating a PR independently.

---

## 31. /loop ΓÇö Schedule Recurring Tasks

Use `/loop` to schedule recurring tasks for up to 3 days at a time. Claude runs your prompt on an interval, handling long-running workflows autonomously.

```
> /loop babysit all my PRs. Auto-fix build issues and when comments come in, use a worktree agent to fix them
```

```
> /loop every morning use the Slack MCP to give me a summary of top posts I was tagged in
```

Use it for PR babysitting, Slack summaries, deploy monitoring, or any repeating workflow.

Learn more: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/scheduled-tasks

## 32. Code Review ΓÇö Agents Hunt for Bugs

When a PR opens, Claude dispatches a team of agents to hunt for bugs. Anthropic built it for themselves first ΓÇö code output per engineer is up 200% this year, and reviews were the bottleneck.

Each agent focuses on a different concern ΓÇö logic errors, security issues, performance regressions ΓÇö then posts inline comments directly on the PR. Boris personally used it for weeks before launch; it catches real bugs he wouldn't have noticed otherwise.

Source: https://x.com/bcherny/status/2031089411820228645

## 33. /btw ΓÇö Ask Questions While Claude Works

A slash command for side-chain conversations while Claude is actively working. Single-turn, no tool calls, but has full context of the conversation.

```
> /btw what does the retry logic do?
```

Claude responds inline without stopping its work. Built by @ErikSchluntz as a side project ΓÇö 1.5M views on the launch tweet.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2031506296697131352

## 34. /effort ΓÇö Max Reasoning Mode

Set effort to 'max' and Claude reasons for longer, using as many tokens as needed. Burns through usage limits faster, so you activate it per session.

```
> /effort max
```

Four levels: low, medium (default), high, max. Use 'max' for hard debugging, architecture decisions, or tricky code where you want Claude to really think it through.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632596572811575

## 35. Remote Control ΓÇö Spawn New Sessions

Run `claude remote-control` and spawn a new local session from the mobile app. Available on Max, Team, and Enterprise (v2.1.74+).

```bash
$ claude remote-control
# Open Claude mobile app ΓåÆ tap "Code" ΓåÆ start new session
```

Walk away from your desk, think of something, kick off a task from mobile ΓÇö Claude runs on your machine.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632597843779861

## 36. Voice Mode

Voice mode is now rolled out to 100% of users, including Claude Code Desktop and Cowork. Click the microphone icon and talk naturally.

Useful for hands-free coding, dictating complex requirements, or when you think faster than you type.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632599429136753

## 37. Setup Scripts for Cloud Environments

Add a setup script in Claude Code on web and desktop. It runs before Claude Code launches on a cloud environment ΓÇö install dependencies, configure settings, set env vars.

```bash
# Setup script (runs on new session start, skipped on resume):
#!/bin/bash
yarn install
```

Particularly useful for installing dependencies, settings, and configs before Claude starts working.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632601064907037

## 38. claude --name ΓÇö Name Your Sessions

Name your session at launch with the `--name` flag.

```bash
$ claude --name "auth-refactor"
```

Especially useful when juggling multiple worktrees or sessions ΓÇö you can tell at a glance which session is doing what.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632602629386348

## 39. Auto Session Naming After Plan Mode

After plan mode, Claude automatically names your session based on what you're working on. No manual naming needed.

Pairs well with `claude --name` ΓÇö use `--name` when you know what you're doing upfront, let auto-naming handle it when you start by planning.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632602629386348

## 40. /color ΓÇö Customize Prompt Color

Change the color of the prompt input with `/color`. When you have 3-5 sessions open in different terminals, color-coding them makes it instantly clear which is which.

```
> /color
```

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632602629386348

## 41. PostCompact Hook

A new hook event that fires after Claude compresses its conversation context. Use it to re-inject critical instructions that might get lost during compaction, log when compaction happens, or trigger automation.

```json
"hooks": {
  "PostCompact": [{
    "matcher": "",
    "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "echo 'Context was compacted'" }]
  }]
}
```

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632602629386348

---

## Quick Reference

| Tip | Key Action |
|-----|------------|
| Parallel work | Use git worktrees, 3-5 sessions |
| Model | Opus with thinking |
| Planning | Start in plan mode for complex tasks |
| CLAUDE.md | Update after every correction |

| Plugins | /plugin for LSPs, MCPs, skills |

| Sandboxing | /sandbox for file & network isolation |
| Status line | /statusline for custom info display |

| Spinners | Customize spinner verbs in settings |
| Output styles | Explanatory, learning, or custom |

| Worktrees | `claude --worktree`, subagent isolation |
| /simplify | Parallel agents for code quality review |
| /batch | Parallel code migrations with worktree isolation |
| /loop | Schedule recurring tasks for up to 3 days |
| Code Review | Agent-powered PR reviews that catch real bugs |
| /btw | Ask questions mid-task without breaking flow |
| /effort | Max reasoning mode for deeper thinking |
| Remote Control | Spawn new sessions from mobile |
| Voice Mode | Talk to Claude Code on Desktop |
| Setup Scripts | Automate cloud environment setup |

| Auto Naming | Plan mode auto-names sessions |

---

content.content:
## Summary

All static content must be rendered from the program JSON definition, not hardcoded in templates. This is foundational ΓÇö without it, program import (#4) and multi-program support (#5) are impossible.

## Content that must be data-driven

- **Sessions & exercises**: names, sets/reps, coaching notes, video links, per-phase overrides
- **Week templates**: schedule options (4-day, 5-day, 6-day) with day-to-session mappings
- **Progressions**: phase-specific coaching adjustments per session
- **Skills**: names, timelines, requirements, gap analysis, milestones
- **Roadmap phases**: week ranges, summaries, gate tests
- **Benchmarks**: names, how-to, targets, retest frequencies
- **Foundations**: breathing protocol steps, deload rules and red flags
- **BODi integration**: recommended programs, hybrid schedules, integration rules (#10)
- **Resources**: YouTube channels, subreddits
- **Theme**: per-program and per-session color theming

## Approach

The program JSON schema (`schema/program.schema.json`) already defines the structure for most of this. Fragments read from the loaded program object ΓÇö never from inline string literals. If content doesn't appear in the schema yet (BODi, resources, foundations), extend the schema first.

## Depends on

- Program JSON schema covering all content areas
- #10 (BODi integration ΓÇö schema extension for external program recommendations)

## Blocks

- #4 (Program import/upload from JSON)
- #5 (Multiple program support)
---SEPARATOR---
Display actual workout data (sets, reps, weights) pulled from Hevy after completion.

Currently, the app only checks whether a workout was completed (binary). This enhancement adds:
- Detailed workout results on completed queue items
- Sets/reps/weight display for each exercise
- Comparison to prescribed sets from the program
- Volume tracking over time

Deferred from v1 because the core value is scheduling, not workout review (Hevy already does this well).
---SEPARATOR---
Add automatic workout completion detection instead of requiring manual refresh.

Currently, completion detection requires the user to open the app or tap "Refresh from Hevy." This enhancement adds:
- Polling interval for Hevy workout API (configurable)
- Or webhook endpoint if Hevy adds webhook support
- Background queue advancement on detected completions
- Push notification when a workout is detected as complete

Deferred from v1 to keep sync user-triggered only and avoid undocumented rate limit issues.
---SEPARATOR---
Support running multiple programs simultaneously or switching between them.

Currently, the app supports a single active program. This enhancement adds:
- Program library view
- Active program switching
- Per-program queue state
- Option to run complementary programs in parallel

Deferred from v1 to keep the data model simple (single active_program per user).
---SEPARATOR---
Allow users to upload their own program JSON files instead of using only the bundled program.

Currently, the program JSON is bundled with the Worker at build time. This enhancement adds:
- Upload UI for program JSON files
- Validation against schema/program.schema.json
- Storage of uploaded programs (D1 or R2)
- Program switching between uploaded and bundled programs

Deferred from v1 to simplify the initial implementation (single bundled program).

input.prompt:
Explore the hevy-planner codebase to understand the current architecture relevant to v2 planning. Specifically:

1. **Program loading**: How is the program JSON currently loaded and used? Look at `src/` for any `loadProgram`, program imports, or bundled program references. How does D1 storage work for programs?

2. **Rendering**: Find all places where content is hardcoded vs data-driven. Look in `src/fragments/` for hardcoded program names, exercise names, or session details that should come from program JSON.

3. **Hevy sync**: Look at `src/hevy/` and any sync-related routes. How does the current sync work? Is there webhook support? How are workouts matched to queue items?

4. **Queue system**: Look at `src/domain/` for queue and reflow logic. How is queue state managed per-program?

5. **Database schema**: Check `migrations/` for current D1 tables, especially anything related to programs, queue_items, and workout data storage.

6. **Route structure**: Check `src/index.ts` for all routes and how they're organized.

Be thorough - read the key files, not just list them. I need to understand the current state to plan v2 work.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me create a summary document of my findings.

## Architecture Deep Dive: hevy-planner v1

Based on my thorough exploration of the codebase, here's the current architecture relevant to v2 planning:

### 1. **Program Loading & Storage**

**Current Implementation:**
- Programs are stored as **complete JSON blobs** in D1: `programs` table with columns `id`, `user_id`, `json_data`, `is_active`, `created_at`
- Loading happens via `loadProgram(db, userId)` which:
  1. Queries `getActiveProgram(db, userId)` ΓåÆ finds the newest active program
  2. Parses the JSON blob: `JSON.parse(row.json_data) as Program`
- Each user can have multiple program versions; only one is marked `is_active = 1`
- Program data loaded **per-request** (no static imports except the bundled default at line 46 of index.ts: `import defaultProgramJson from "../programs/mobility-joint-restoration.json"`)
- Setup flow (`handleSetup` in index.ts:478-648) validates, stores, and syncs the entire program in one transaction

**Key Path:** `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:80-84` (loadProgram)

### 2. **Rendering: Hardcoded vs Data-Driven**

**Current State: Nearly Fully Data-Driven**
- All fragments in `src/fragments/` take program data as parameters and render dynamically
- **Daily routine handling** is special:
  - Marked by `isDaily: boolean` on Routine type (src/types.ts:96)
  - Found via: `program.routines.find((r) => r.isDaily)` (index.ts:312, 867)
  - Hardcoded UI label: `"Daily"` color label with `var(--green)` (today.ts:22)
  - Hardcoded route: `/routine/daily` deep link (today.ts:27)

**Hardcoded Elements Found:**
1. Daily routine ID: route hardcodes `"daily"` in carsCard button: `@post('/api/push-hevy/daily')` (today.ts:19)
2. Label text: "Daily", "Next Session", "CARs only" (today.ts, reflow.ts:55)
3. Folder names: "Daily" folder for isDaily routines (hevy-sync.ts:117)

**All else is data-driven:**
- Exercise names, titles, descriptions ΓåÆ from exerciseTemplates + routines
- Routine counts, exercise counts ΓåÆ computed from program.routines
- Phase info ΓåÆ from program.progressions
- Upcoming sessions ΓåÆ computeUpcoming() algorithm uses template rhythm + queue state

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/today.ts:13-29` (carsCard with hardcoded "daily")
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/routine-detail.ts:13-87` (fully data-driven)
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/program.ts:79-130+` (routines section)

### 3. **Hevy Sync Architecture**

**Two-way Sync with Reconciliation:**

**Push (Setup + On-Demand):**
1. `handleSetup` (index.ts:478-648):
   - Creates or updates Hevy exercise templates (auto-match by name, then custom creation)
   - Creates folders via `getOrCreateRoutineFolder()`
   - Reconciles routines: checks D1 mappings first, then matches by title+folder in Hevy, then creates new
   - Stores 1:1 mappings in D1: `exercise_template_mappings` + `routine_mappings`
   
2. `handlePush` (index.ts:650-750):
   - For ad-hoc routine pushes (user clicks "Push to Hevy" on a detail page)

- Loading happens via `loadProgram(db, userId)` which:

input.prompt:
Design an execution plan for the v2 milestone of hevy-planner, following Boris Cherny's Claude Code workflow principles: parallel worktrees, plan-then-1-shot, verification at every step, and /simplify after changes.

## v2 Issues (5 total)

### #9 ΓÇö Data-driven rendering
All static content rendered from program JSON, not hardcoded. Foundational ΓÇö blocks #4 and #5.
Current state: Nearly fully data-driven already. Remaining hardcoded items:
- Daily routine ID: routes/buttons use "daily" string literal (today.ts:19, index.ts route `/api/push-hevy/daily`)
- Label text: "Daily", "Next Session", "CARs only" (today.ts, reflow.ts:55)
- Folder names: "Daily" folder for isDaily routines (hevy-sync.ts:117)
- Daily routine color: hardcoded `var(--green)` (today.ts:22)
Key files: src/fragments/today.ts, src/domain/reflow.ts, src/domain/hevy-sync.ts, src/index.ts

### #7 ΓÇö Workout detail display (sets/reps/weights from Hevy)
Show actual workout data on completed queue items. Currently binary (done/not done).
Needs: Hevy workout data storage, display in fragments, comparison to prescribed sets.
Key files: src/hevy/client.ts, src/storage/queries.ts, src/fragments/today.ts, src/index.ts (handlePull)

### #6 ΓÇö Webhook-based auto-sync
Replace manual "Sync from Hevy" with webhooks. Hevy MCP tools include create-webhook-subscription, get-webhook-subscription, delete-webhook-subscription, get-workout-events.
Needs: webhook endpoint route, event processing, queue advancement.
Key files: src/index.ts, src/domain/hevy-sync.ts, src/hevy/client.ts

### #4 ΓÇö Program import/upload from JSON
Upload UI for program JSON, validate against schema, store in D1.
Currently: only bundled program loaded at setup. D1 `programs` table already stores JSON blobs.
Needs: upload UI fragment, validation endpoint, program storage, re-setup flow.
Key files: src/index.ts, src/fragments/, src/storage/queries.ts, schema/program.schema.json

### #5 ΓÇö Multiple program support
Program library, active switching, per-program queue state.
Currently: single active_program per user. Queue items reference routine_id (string from program JSON).
Needs: program list page, switching logic, queue scoped to program, parallel program option.
Key files: All of the above + new fragments, domain logic changes.

## Dependency Graph
```
#9 (data-driven) ΓöÇΓöÇΓåÆ #4 (import) ΓöÇΓöÇΓåÆ #5 (multi-program)
#7 (workout details) ΓÇö independent
#6 (webhooks) ΓÇö independent
```

## Current Architecture Context
- Cloudflare Workers + D1 + Datastar SSE
- Programs stored as JSON blobs in D1 `programs` table
- Queue: flat `queue_items` table with position-based ordering
- Hevy sync: push (setup) + pull (manual button), reconciliation via D1 mappings
- All domain logic pure functions in src/domain/
- Fragments return HTML strings with Datastar attributes
- 47 tests passing
- Content negotiation via Accept header (SSE vs HTML)

## Boris Method Requirements
1. **Parallel worktrees**: Identify which issues can be worked simultaneously
2. **Plan ΓåÆ 1-shot**: Each issue needs enough detail for a single implementation pass
3. **Verification**: Define how to test each change (tests, MCP tools, manual checks)
4. **/simplify**: Run after each issue to clean up

Design the execution plan with:
- Wave structure (what runs in parallel vs sequential)
- For each issue: scope, key changes, files modified, verification steps
- Migration needs (D1 schema changes)
- Risk areas and gotchas

Design an execution plan for the v2 milestone of hevy-planner, following Boris Cherny's Claude Code workflow principles: parallel worktrees, plan-then-1-shot, verification at every step, and /simplify after changes.

## v2 Issues (5 total)

### #9 ΓÇö Data-driven rendering
All static content rendered from program JSON, not hardcoded. Foundational ΓÇö blocks #4 and #5.
Current state: Nearly fully data-driven already. Remaining hardcoded items:
- Daily routine ID: routes/buttons use "daily" string literal (today.ts:19, index.ts route `/api/push-hevy/daily`)

- Folder names: "Daily" folder for isDaily routines (hevy-sync.ts:117)

### #7 ΓÇö Workout detail display (sets/reps/weights from Hevy)
Show actual workout data on completed queue items. Currently binary (done/not done).
Needs: Hevy workout data storage, display in fragments, comparison to prescribed sets.

Replace manual "Sync from Hevy" with webhooks. Hevy MCP tools include create-webhook-subscription, get-webhook-subscription, delete-webhook-subscription, get-workout-events.
Needs: webhook endpoint route, event processing, queue advancement.
Key files: src/index.ts, src/domain/hevy-sync.ts, src/hevy/client.ts

content.content:
1ΓåÆ# Product Spec
     2ΓåÆ
     3ΓåÆ## What Is This?
     4ΓåÆ
     5ΓåÆA training companion that adds the planning intelligence Hevy doesn't have. It tells you what to do today, automatically adjusts your schedule when you miss a workout, tracks long-term skill progression, and manages benchmark testing across a multi-phase roadmap ΓÇö all built around programs you can define, import, and share.
     6ΓåÆ
     7ΓåÆHevy handles the workout itself: exercise logging, Apple Watch, sets/reps, PRs, history. This companion handles everything upstream and downstream: scheduling, reflow, phase management, and program definition.
     8ΓåÆ
     9ΓåÆIt runs as a web app hosted on Cloudflare Workers, accessed from any browser or as a PWA on your phone's home screen. It syncs with Hevy via the Hevy API.
    10ΓåÆ
    11ΓåÆ### Goals
    12ΓåÆ
    13ΓåÆ- Show the user exactly what to do today, in order, with coaching context
    14ΓåÆ- Automatically reflow the schedule when a workout is missed
    15ΓåÆ- Track progress across a multi-phase roadmap (weeks to months to a year)
    16ΓåÆ- Gate advancement between phases on measurable benchmarks
    17ΓåÆ- Track long-term skill goals (muscle-up, handstand, etc.) with milestone progression
    18ΓåÆ- Push today's routine to Hevy so it's ready when you open the app
    19ΓåÆ- Pull completion data from Hevy to advance the queue
    20ΓåÆ- Define programs via an open JSON schema that anyone can create and share
    21ΓåÆ
    22ΓåÆ### Non-Goals (v1)
    23ΓåÆ
    24ΓåÆ- Replacing Hevy for workout logging (sets, reps, rest timers, PRs)
    25ΓåÆ- Building native iOS or watchOS apps
    26ΓåÆ- AI-generated programming or adaptive difficulty
    27ΓåÆ- Social features
    28ΓåÆ
    29ΓåÆ---
    30ΓåÆ
    31ΓåÆ## How It Works With Hevy
    32ΓåÆ
    33ΓåÆ### Sync Model
    34ΓåÆ
    35ΓåÆ```
    36ΓåÆ   Companion (Cloudflare)              Hevy
    37ΓåÆ   ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇ              ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇ
    38ΓåÆ   Program loaded from JSON
    39ΓåÆ   Queue generated from template
    40ΓåÆ   "Today: CARs + Session B"
    41ΓåÆ            Γöé
    42ΓåÆ            Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇpush routineΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓû║  Routine appears in Hevy
    43ΓåÆ            Γöé                       User opens Hevy, does workout
    44ΓåÆ            Γöé                       Logs sets/reps on phone or Watch
    45ΓåÆ            Γöé
    46ΓåÆ            ΓùäΓöÇΓöÇpull completionΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇ  Workout marked complete
    47ΓåÆ            Γöé
    48ΓåÆ   Queue advances
    49ΓåÆ   Reflow if needed
    50ΓåÆ   Next day's routine ready
    51ΓåÆ```
    52ΓåÆ
    53ΓåÆ### Hevy API Integration
    54ΓåÆ
    55ΓåÆThe [Hevy API](https://api.hevyapp.com/docs/) (requires Pro subscription) provides:
    56ΓåÆ
    57ΓåÆ- **Routines**: Create, update, and list workout routines. The companion pushes today's session as a Hevy routine.
    58ΓåÆ- **Workouts**: List completed workouts with full exercise/set detail. The companion polls for completions to advance the queue.
    59ΓåÆ- **Exercises**: List available exercises and exercise templates. Used to map program exercises to Hevy's exercise library.
    60ΓåÆ- **Routine Folders**: Organize routines by program or phase.
    61ΓåÆ
    62ΓåÆ### Sync Triggers
    63ΓåÆ
    64ΓåÆ- **On page load**: Pull recent workouts from Hevy, reconcile with queue state
    65ΓåÆ- **On "Push to Hevy" tap**: Create or update today's routine in Hevy
    66ΓåÆ- **On manual refresh**: Re-pull from Hevy to check for new completions
    67ΓåÆ- **Future**: Webhook or polling interval for automatic sync
    68ΓåÆ
    69ΓåÆ---
    70ΓåÆ
    71ΓåÆ## Screens
    72ΓåÆ
    73ΓåÆ### 1. Today (Home)
    74ΓåÆ
    75ΓåÆWhat you see when you open the app. Shows today's queue in order.
    76ΓåÆ

   280ΓåÆ
   281ΓåÆ## User Flows
   282ΓåÆ
   283ΓåÆ### First Launch / Setup
   284ΓåÆ
   285ΓåÆ1. Open the app URL in browser (or install as PWA)
   286ΓåÆ2. Connect Hevy: enter API key (from Hevy Settings > Developer)
   287ΓåÆ3. Load a program: select bundled program or import from JSON file
   288ΓåÆ4. Pick a start date (defaults to today)
   289ΓåÆ5. Pick a schedule template (4-day, 5-day, 6-day)
   290ΓåÆ6. Land on Today screen with the first day's queue populated
   291ΓåÆ
   292ΓåÆ### Daily Use
   293ΓåÆ
   294ΓåÆ1. Open companion ΓåÆ Today screen shows queue (CARs + main session)
   295ΓåÆ2. Review session detail and coaching notes if needed
   296ΓåÆ3. Tap "Push to Hevy" ΓåÆ routine appears in Hevy
   297ΓåÆ4. Open Hevy on phone or Watch, do the workout, log sets/reps
   298ΓåÆ5. Return to companion (or it auto-syncs) ΓåÆ workout detected as complete
   299ΓåÆ6. Queue advances, next item updates, week overview reflects completion
   300ΓåÆ
   301ΓåÆ### Missed Workout
   302ΓåÆ
   303ΓåÆ1. It's Thursday. Wednesday's Session B was not completed.
   304ΓåÆ2. Open the companion Thursday morning.
   305ΓåÆ3. Today screen shows Thursday's normal schedule (CARs + strength note).
   306ΓåÆ4. Session B has been re-enqueued to the first open day this week.
   307ΓåÆ5. Week overview shows Wednesday as missed.
   308ΓåÆ6. Friday proceeds normally with its scheduled session.
   309ΓåÆ
   310ΓåÆ### Benchmark Testing
   311ΓåÆ
   312ΓåÆ1. Benchmarks screen shows "Wall Dorsiflexion Test ΓÇö due this week"
   313ΓåÆ2. User performs the test, taps "Log Result", enters measurement
   314ΓåÆ3. Result is saved, trend updates, gate test status recalculated
   315ΓåÆ4. If all gate tests for current roadmap phase pass, user is prompted to advance
   316ΓåÆ
   317ΓåÆ### Phase Advancement
   318ΓåÆ
   319ΓåÆ1. All Phase 1 gate benchmarks pass
   320ΓåÆ2. Roadmap screen shows "Phase 1 complete ΓÇö ready to advance?"
   321ΓåÆ3. User confirms ΓåÆ Phase 2 becomes active
   322ΓåÆ4. New sessions/exercises are added to the queue from the Phase 2 program definition
   323ΓåÆ5. Schedule template may update (or user is prompted to choose a new one)
   324ΓåÆ
   325ΓåÆ---
   326ΓåÆ
   327ΓåÆ## Queue & Reflow Rules
   328ΓåÆ
   329ΓåÆSame rules as before ΓÇö the queue is the core scheduling engine:
   330ΓåÆ
   331ΓåÆ1. Each day, the queue is populated from the active template. **Daily sessions** are inserted first, followed by the day's scheduled session(s), in template order.
   332ΓåÆ
   333ΓåÆ2. The user works through items in sequence. The **next item** is always the first incomplete item in today's queue.
   334ΓåÆ
   335ΓåÆ3. When Hevy reports an item as **completed**, it's marked done and the queue advances.
   336ΓåÆ
   337ΓåÆ4. **Daily sessions have special reset behavior**: never reflowed when missed, just dropped. A fresh daily entry is inserted at the top of the next day's queue.
   338ΓåÆ
   339ΓåÆ5. **Non-daily sessions follow reflow rules**: when a scheduled date passes without completion, the session is re-enqueued after the remaining scheduled sessions for the week. If no open day remains, it's dropped for the week.
   340ΓåÆ
   341ΓåÆ6. At the **end of each week**, any remaining skipped sessions are dropped. The next week's queue is generated fresh from the template.
   342ΓåÆ
   343ΓåÆ---
   344ΓåÆ
   345ΓåÆ## Data Schema
   346ΓåÆ
   347ΓåÆPrograms are defined via the JSON Schema in `schema/program.schema.json`. The schema covers:
   348ΓåÆ
   349ΓåÆ- **Program metadata**: title, author, duration, tags
   350ΓåÆ- **Sessions**: named groups of exercises, with an `isDaily` flag for daily-reset behavior
   351ΓåÆ- **Exercises**: name, sets/reps, coaching notes, video links, cross-session tags, per-phase overrides
   352ΓåÆ- **Week templates**: multiple schedule options (e.g., 4-day, 5-day, 6-day)
   353ΓåÆ- **Progressions**: phase-based coaching adjustments within the program
   354ΓåÆ- **Roadmap phases**: long-term phases with gate tests that reference benchmarks
   355ΓåÆ- **Skills**: long-term goals with milestones and timelines
   356ΓåÆ- **Benchmarks**: measurable assessments with targets and retest frequencies
   357ΓåÆ- **Foundations**: protocols (breathing, bracing) that apply across the program
   358ΓåÆ- **Theme**: optional per-program and per-session color theming
   359ΓåÆ
   360ΓåÆSee `schema/program.schema.json` for the full definition.
   361ΓåÆ
   362ΓåÆ### User Data (persisted in D1)
   363ΓåÆ
   364ΓåÆ```
   365ΓåÆUserState
   366ΓåÆ  userID: string
   367ΓåÆ  activeProgramID: string
   368ΓåÆ  activeTemplateID: string
   369ΓåÆ  startDate: date
   370ΓåÆ  currentRoadmapPhaseID: string
   371ΓåÆ  hevyAPIKey: string (encrypted)
   372ΓåÆ
   373ΓåÆQueueItem
   374ΓåÆ  sessionID: string

   381ΓåÆ
   382ΓåÆBenchmarkResult
   383ΓåÆ  benchmarkID: string

   394ΓåÆ
   395ΓåÆ### Hevy Exercise Mapping
   396ΓåÆ
   397ΓåÆPrograms define exercises by name. The companion maps these to Hevy's exercise library when pushing routines. A mapping table in D1 stores confirmed matches:
   398ΓåÆ
   399ΓåÆ```
   400ΓåÆExerciseMapping
   401ΓåÆ  programExerciseID: string
   402ΓåÆ  hevyExerciseID: string
   403ΓåÆ  confirmedByUser: bool
   404ΓåÆ```
   405ΓåÆ
   406ΓåÆOn first push, the companion attempts to auto-match by name. Unmatched exercises are flagged for the user to resolve manually.
   407ΓåÆ
   408ΓåÆ---
   409ΓåÆ
   410ΓåÆ## Authentication
   411ΓåÆ
   412ΓåÆThe app is protected by [Cloudflare Access](https://www.cloudflare.com/zero-trust/products/access/), which handles authentication at the edge before any request reaches the Worker. There is no application-level login flow, session management, or password storage.
   413ΓåÆ
   414ΓåÆ**How it works**: Cloudflare Access acts as an identity-aware reverse proxy. Users authenticate through a configured identity provider (email OTP, GitHub, Google, etc.) and receive a signed JWT. The Worker validates this token on every request ΓÇö unauthenticated requests never reach application code.
   415ΓåÆ
   416ΓåÆ**Access policy**: An allowlist of permitted email addresses (starting with the owner's). Adding new users means adding an email to the Cloudflare Access policy ΓÇö no application changes required.
   417ΓåÆ
   418ΓåÆ**What the Worker does**:
   419ΓåÆ
   420ΓåÆ- Validates the `Cf-Access-Jwt-Assertion` header on every request
   421ΓåÆ- Extracts the authenticated user identity from the token claims
   422ΓåÆ- Uses the identity to scope all D1 queries (each user sees only their own data)
   423ΓåÆ- Rejects requests with missing or invalid tokens (403)
   424ΓåÆ
   425ΓåÆ**What the Worker does NOT do**:
   426ΓåÆ
   427ΓåÆ- Store passwords or credentials
   428ΓåÆ- Manage login/logout sessions
   429ΓåÆ- Implement OAuth flows
   430ΓåÆ- Handle user registration
   431ΓåÆ
   432ΓåÆ**Cost**: Cloudflare Access is free for up to 50 users. This is more than sufficient for personal use and early open-source adoption.
   433ΓåÆ
   434ΓåÆ---
   435ΓåÆ
   436ΓåÆ## Settings
   437ΓåÆ
   438ΓåÆ- **Hevy Connection**: API key management, sync status, last sync time
   439ΓåÆ- **Template**: Switch between schedule templates (regenerates future queue items)
   440ΓåÆ- **Start Date**: Adjust if needed (recalculates current week and phase)
   441ΓåÆ- **Program**: View active program, import new program from JSON
   442ΓåÆ- **Reset Program**: Start over from week 1 (confirmation required)
   443ΓåÆ
   444ΓåÆ---
   445ΓåÆ
   446ΓåÆ## Prerequisites & Costs
   447ΓåÆ
   448ΓåÆ### What You Need
   449ΓåÆ
   450ΓåÆ- **Hevy Pro subscription** ($9.99/month or $49.99/year) ΓÇö required for API access. Get your API key from [Hevy Settings > Developer](https://hevy.com/settings?developer). [Hevy app](https://www.hevyapp.com/).
   451ΓåÆ- **Cloudflare account** (free tier is sufficient to start) ΓÇö [Sign up](https://dash.cloudflare.com/sign-up).
   452ΓåÆ- **Node.js 18+** ΓÇö for local development and the Wrangler CLI.
   453ΓåÆ- **Wrangler CLI** ΓÇö Cloudflare's deployment tool (`npm install -g wrangler`).
   454ΓåÆ
   455ΓåÆ### Cloudflare Costs
   456ΓåÆ
   457ΓåÆThe free tier covers most personal use:
   458ΓåÆ
   459ΓåÆ| Resource | Free Tier | Notes |
   460ΓåÆ|----------|-----------|-------|
   461ΓåÆ| Workers requests | 100,000/day | More than enough for a personal app |
   462ΓåÆ| D1 database | 5 GB storage, 5M rows read/day | Workout history for years |
   463ΓåÆ| KV (if used) | 100,000 reads/day | Not required for v1 |
   464ΓåÆ| Custom domain | Bring your own | Optional; `*.workers.dev` subdomain is free |
   465ΓåÆ
   466ΓåÆ**Estimated cost for personal use: $0/month** beyond the Hevy Pro subscription.
   467ΓåÆ
   468ΓåÆIf usage grows (multiple users, heavy API polling), the Workers Paid plan is $5/month and covers 10M requests/month with higher D1 limits.
   469ΓåÆ
   470ΓåÆ### Hevy API Limits
   471ΓåÆ
   472ΓåÆThe Hevy API documentation does not publish explicit rate limits. The app should implement conservative backoff/retry logic and cache aggressively. For a single user syncing a few times per day, rate limits are unlikely to be an issue.
   473ΓåÆ
   474ΓåÆ---
   475ΓåÆ
   476ΓåÆ## Decisions Log
   477ΓåÆ
   478ΓåÆ| # | Decision | Rationale |
   479ΓåÆ|---|----------|-----------|
   480ΓåÆ| 1 | Companion to Hevy, not a standalone tracker | Hevy already has native apps, Watch support, exercise logging, PRs. Don't rebuild what works. |
   481ΓåÆ| 2 | Cloudflare Workers + Datastar | The entire product is a web app. Datastar's SSE-driven hypermedia is a natural fit. No native apps needed. |
   482ΓåÆ| 3 | TypeScript on Workers | First-class Cloudflare support. Datastar TS SDK works directly. Claude knows it deeply for agent-assisted builds. |
   483ΓåÆ| 4 | D1 for persistence | Relational queries for queue state, workout history, benchmark trends. SQLite at the edge. |
   484ΓåÆ| 5 | Programs defined via JSON Schema | Open, shareable, community-extensible. Anyone can create a program. |
   485ΓåÆ| 6 | Daily sessions are queue items that reset daily | Unified queue model. Daily items always appear first, never reflowed. |
   486ΓåÆ| 7 | Hevy API for sync, not scraping or manual entry | Official API with routine and workout CRUD. Requires Pro subscription. |
   487ΓåÆ| 8 | PWA for mobile access | No App Store needed. Install from browser. Works on any device with a browser. |
   488ΓåÆ| 9 | Cloudflare Access for authentication | Zero application auth code. Identity handled at the edge. Free for up to 50 users. |

I now have a thorough understanding of the entire codebase. Let me compile the full execution plan.

---

# v2 Execution Plan for hevy-planner

## Architecture Summary (Current State)

The codebase is a Cloudflare Worker serving Datastar SSE-driven HTML. There are approximately 12 source files, 47 tests (vitest), and 4 D1 migrations. The program JSON blob is stored in `programs` table and loaded per-request. A single `users` table holds per-user state including `active_program` (text label, not FK), `template_id`, `start_date`, and `hevy_api_key`. Queue items are a flat table with position-based ordering. Routine and exercise template mappings bridge the program JSON to Hevy API entity IDs.

---

## Wave Structure

### Wave 1 (parallel): #9 Data-Driven + #7 Workout Details + #6 Webhooks

These three issues have zero dependencies on each other. Each should be in its own worktree branching from `main`.

### Wave 2 (sequential from #9): #4 Program Import

Depends on #9 being merged first so that all rendering derives from program JSON, not hardcoded strings. The upload/validate flow already exists in skeleton form (setup page has file upload + `/api/validate-program`), so this is mostly completing the non-setup path (upload a new program while already set up).

### Wave 3 (sequential from #4): #5 Multiple Programs

Depends on #4 because switching programs means importing a second one. Requires schema migration and the most domain logic changes.

---

## Issue #9 -- Data-Driven Rendering

**Branch**: `feat/data-driven-rendering`

**Goal**: Eliminate every hardcoded reference to specific routine IDs, labels, colors, and folder names. All display and routing must derive from the `Program` JSON blob stored in D1.

**Scope of Changes (small -- ~5 files)**:

1. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/today.ts`** -- The `carsCard` function:
   - Line 19: hardcodes `@post('/api/push-hevy/daily')` -- should use `routine.id` from the passed-in `Routine` object instead of the literal string `"daily"`.
   - Line 22: hardcodes `style="color: var(--green)"` -- should use `routine.color ?? "var(--green)"` (the actual program JSON has `"color": "rgb(96, 165, 224)"` for the daily routine).
   - Line 22: hardcodes `Daily` as the card label -- should derive from program JSON. Options: (a) add a `cardLabel` field to the Routine schema, or (b) use `routine.isDaily ? "Daily" : "Next Session"` which is still data-driven (isDaily comes from JSON). Option (b) is simpler and avoids schema changes. The label logic should be extracted to a pure function in domain.
   - Line 27: hardcodes `/routine/daily` -- should use `/routine/${encodeURIComponent(routine.id)}`.

2. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts`** -- Route handler:
   - The route `/api/push-hevy/:id` already accepts any ID dynamically, so no route changes needed.
   - In `handleTodaySSE` (line 312): `program.routines.find((r) => r.isDaily)` -- this is already data-driven (reads `isDaily` from JSON). No change needed here.

3. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/reflow.ts`** -- Line 55:
   - Hardcodes the string `"CARs only"` for spacer days. This should come from the daily routine's title or a configurable label. Approach: pass the daily routine's title (if one exists) to `computeUpcoming` and use it for spacer labels. Signature change: `computeUpcoming(pendingItems, template, routines, maxSessions)` gains no new param -- it already has `routines`, so it can find the daily routine and use its title.

4. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/hevy-sync.ts`** -- Line 117:
   - Hardcodes `"Daily"` as the default folder name for `isDaily` routines. The actual program JSON already has `"folderGroup": "Daily"` on the daily routine, so this fallback only triggers when `folderGroup` is missing. Two options: (a) keep `"Daily"` as a reasonable default -- it is a category label, not a program-specific string; (b) derive from routine title. Option (a) is acceptable since `folderGroup` exists in the schema and programs should set it. Document that `"Daily"` is the conventional default for `isDaily` routines when `folderGroup` is omitted.

5. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/today.ts`** -- The `heroRoutineCard` function:
   - Line 48: hardcodes `"Next Session"` as the card label. Should use a derived label. Since the hero card is always a non-daily routine from the queue, `"Next Session"` is actually functional, not program-specific. However, for full data-driven rendering, the label could come from a `cardLabel` field on the routine, or remain as `"Next Session"` since it describes queue position, not content. Decision: keep `"Next Session"` -- it is UI chrome describing position in the queue, not program content.

**New tests**:
- Unit test for `computeUpcoming` verifying spacer label derives from daily routine title, not hardcoded string.
- Unit test verifying `carsCard` output uses `routine.color` and `routine.id`, not hardcoded values (render the function and check the HTML string contains the expected color/URL).

**Verification**:
- `npm run test` -- all existing + new tests pass.
- `wrangler dev` -- visually verify Today page renders with program-defined colors and labels.
- Grep the entire `src/` directory for the string literal `"daily"` (with quotes) and `"Daily"` -- confirm they only appear where they are defaults/fallbacks, not primary rendering paths.

**`/simplify` targets**: After the change, `carsCard` and `heroRoutineCard` can potentially be unified into a single `routineCard` function that takes a mode/position parameter. Evaluate during simplify pass.

---

## Issue #7 -- Workout Detail Display

**Branch**: `feat/workout-details`

**Goal**: When a queue item is completed via Hevy sync, store the actual workout data (exercises, sets, reps, weights) and display it on the Today page and in a detail view.

**Migration needed**: `migrations/0005_workout_data.sql`

```sql
ALTER TABLE queue_items ADD COLUMN hevy_workout_data TEXT;
```

This column stores the JSON blob of the workout's exercise/set detail from Hevy. Stored as text, parsed on read. This avoids a separate table and keeps the relationship simple (1:1 with queue item). The Hevy workout response already includes full exercise/set detail in `HevyWorkout.exercises`.

**Scope of Changes**:

1. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/hevy/client.ts`** -- No structural changes needed. `HevyWorkout` already includes `exercises` with full set data. The `getRecentWorkouts` method already returns this data. Just ensure the type `HevyWorkout.exercises[].sets` includes `weight_kg` (it does via `HevySet`).

2. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/storage/queries.ts`** -- Modify `markQueueItemCompleted` to accept and store workout data:
   - Add `workoutData?: string` parameter.
   - Update SQL to `SET ... hevy_workout_data = ?`.

3. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/types.ts`** -- Add `hevy_workout_data` to `QueueItemRow`:
   ```typescript
   readonly hevy_workout_data: string | null;
   ```

4. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts`** -- In `handlePull`, when a match is found, serialize the workout's exercises as JSON and pass to `markQueueItemCompleted`:
   - Find the matching workout from `newWorkouts` by ID.
   - `JSON.stringify(workout.exercises)` and store it.

5. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/today.ts`** -- Enhance `completedSection`:
   - Change the `items` type from `Array<{ title: string }>` to include optional workout data.
   - For items with workout data, render a summary row: exercise count, total sets, any weights used.
   - Add an expandable detail showing each exercise with actual sets/reps/weights vs. the prescribed sets from the routine.

6. **New fragment function**: `workoutSummary(workoutData: HevyWorkout['exercises'], prescribed: RoutineExercise[])` -- Pure function that compares actual vs. prescribed and returns HTML.

7. **New domain function**: `compareWorkout(actual: HevyWorkout['exercises'], prescribed: RoutineExercise[])` in `src/domain/workout-compare.ts`:
   - Maps exercises by `exercise_template_id`.
   - Returns an array of `{ exerciseTitle, prescribedSets, actualSets, status: 'matched' | 'extra' | 'missing' }`.

**Verification**:
- Migration applies cleanly: `wrangler d1 execute hevy-planner --local --file=migrations/0005_workout_data.sql`.
- Unit tests for `compareWorkout` domain function.
- Manual test: complete a workout in Hevy, hit "Sync from Hevy", verify workout data appears in the completed section.
- Verify completed items without workout data (manual completes) still render correctly (graceful null handling).

**Risk areas**:
- HevyWorkout response size: a workout with 10 exercises and 5 sets each is roughly 2-3KB JSON. Stored in D1 TEXT column, well within limits.
- Exercise template ID matching between the stored workout data and the program's exercise templates requires the `exercise_template_mappings` table. The workout data from Hevy uses Hevy template IDs, while the program uses program template IDs. The comparison function needs to map via the mappings table (or accept a pre-built map).

---

## Issue #6 -- Webhook-Based Auto-Sync

**Branch**: `feat/webhooks`

**Goal**: Replace the manual "Sync from Hevy" button with automatic sync triggered by Hevy webhooks when workouts are completed.

**Hevy Webhook API** (from MCP tool definitions):
- `create-webhook-subscription(url, authToken?)` -- registers a URL to receive POST when workouts are created. Must respond 200 within 5 seconds.
- `get-webhook-subscription()` -- returns current subscription.
- `delete-webhook-subscription()` -- removes subscription.
- `get-workout-events(since, page, pageSize)` -- paged list of workout updates/deletes since a date.

**Key design decision**: Hevy webhooks notify when a workout is *created* (completed). The webhook POST contains event data. The Worker endpoint must process it within 5 seconds (Cloudflare Workers have a 30-second CPU limit, so this is fine).

**Migration needed**: `migrations/0005_webhook_state.sql` (or 0006 if #7 takes 0005)

```sql
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN webhook_id TEXT;
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN webhook_auth_token TEXT;
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_sync_at TEXT;
```

**Scope of Changes**:

1. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/hevy/client.ts`** -- Add webhook methods:
   - `createWebhookSubscription(url: string, authToken?: string): Promise<{ id: string }>`.
   - `getWebhookSubscription(): Promise<{ url: string; id: string } | null>`.
   - `deleteWebhookSubscription(): Promise<void>`.
   - `getWorkoutEvents(since: string, page?: number): Promise<WorkoutEvent[]>`.

2. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/types.ts`** -- Add `webhook_id`, `webhook_auth_token`, `last_sync_at` to `UserRow`.

3. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/storage/queries.ts`** -- Add:
   - `updateWebhookState(db, userId, webhookId, authToken)`.
   - `updateLastSyncAt(db, userId, timestamp)`.
   - Update `upsertUser` to handle new columns.

4. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts`** -- New routes:
   - `POST /api/webhooks/hevy` -- Incoming webhook endpoint. Validates auth token from header against stored token. Runs the same sync logic as `handlePull`. Returns 200 immediately. The challenge: the webhook fires for *all* users of the Hevy account, but this is a single-user app effectively (one API key per user). The webhook URL includes no user context, so the endpoint needs to look up the user by the auth token.
   - `POST /api/webhooks/register` -- UI action to register the webhook. Generates a random auth token, calls Hevy API to register, stores the webhook ID and token.
   - `POST /api/webhooks/unregister` -- Removes the webhook subscription.

5. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/today.ts`** -- Replace the sync button:
   - If webhook is registered: show "Auto-sync enabled" badge instead of the manual sync button.
   - Keep manual sync as a fallback regardless (in case webhook was missed).
   - Show `last_sync_at` timestamp.

6. **Webhook auth design**: Generate a random UUID as the auth token when registering. Store it in the users table. When the webhook fires, the Worker receives the token in the `Authorization` header, queries users by that token to find the user, then runs sync for that user.

**Verification**:
- Register webhook via MCP tool or UI, verify subscription exists via `get-webhook-subscription`.
- Complete a workout in Hevy, verify the webhook fires and the queue advances without manual sync.
- Test the manual sync button still works as fallback.
- Test auth token validation: send a webhook with wrong token, verify 401 response.

**Risk areas**:
- Webhook URL must be publicly accessible. During development, use `wrangler dev --remote` or deploy to staging.
- Hevy may retry failed webhooks (undocumented). Ensure idempotency: the existing `usedWorkoutIds` filter in `handlePull` already handles this.
- 5-second response requirement: the sync logic involves D1 queries and possibly Hevy API calls (`getRecentWorkouts`). In the webhook handler, consider doing a lightweight acknowledgment (200 OK) and processing asynchronously via `waitUntil` (available in Cloudflare Workers).
- Multi-user: if multiple users share the same Worker, each needs their own webhook subscription. The auth token lookup resolves which user to sync.

---

## Issue #4 -- Program Import/Upload from JSON

**Branch**: `feat/program-import` (after #9 is merged)

**Goal**: Allow users who already have an active program to upload a new program JSON, validate it, and replace their active program.

**No migration needed** -- the `programs` table already supports multiple rows per user with `is_active` flag, and `insertProgram` already deactivates previous programs.

**Scope of Changes**:

1. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/program.ts`** -- Add an "Import Program" section at the bottom of the Program page:
   - File upload input (reuse the pattern from `setupPage`).
   - Validate button.
   - Template selection (appears after validation).
   - "Apply" action that replaces the active program.

2. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts`** -- New route:
   - `POST /api/import-program` -- Accepts program JSON + template ID. Validates, stores in `programs` table (deactivating old), regenerates queue, re-syncs Hevy routines. This is essentially `handleSetup` minus the API key step (reuse existing key from user record). Refactor common logic from `handleSetup` into a shared `activateProgram(db, userId, program, templateId, apiKey?)` function.

3. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/storage/queries.ts`** -- Add:
   - `clearQueueItems(db, userId)` -- Delete all pending queue items for user (before regenerating).
   - `clearMappings(db, userId)` -- Clear exercise template and routine mappings (they are program-specific).

4. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/queue.ts`** -- No changes. `generatePlaylist` is already pure and reusable.

5. **Validation**: Already exists via `validateProgram`. The schema at `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/schema/program.schema.json` is compiled to `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/compiled-validator.mjs`.

**Verification**:
- Upload a different valid program JSON, verify it replaces the active program.
- Verify the queue is regenerated from the new program.
- Verify Hevy routines are updated/created for the new program.
- Verify uploading an invalid JSON shows validation errors.
- Verify the old program's queue items are cleared.

**Risk areas**:
- Queue items that were already completed should be preserved for history. Decision: only clear *pending* items. Completed items reference routine IDs from the old program, which is fine for historical display.
- Exercise template mappings from the old program may conflict with the new one if template IDs collide. Decision: clear all mappings on import and re-create them.

---

## Issue #5 -- Multiple Program Support

**Branch**: `feat/multi-program` (after #4 is merged)

**Goal**: Support a library of programs with the ability to switch between them. Each program has its own queue state.

**Migration needed**: `migrations/0006_multi_program.sql` (or 0007 depending on numbering)

```sql
-- Add program_id FK to queue_items to scope queue per program
ALTER TABLE queue_items ADD COLUMN program_id INTEGER REFERENCES programs(id);

-- Backfill: set program_id to the user's current active program
UPDATE queue_items SET program_id = (
  SELECT p.id FROM programs p
  WHERE p.user_id = queue_items.user_id AND p.is_active = 1
  ORDER BY p.created_at DESC LIMIT 1
);

-- Add program_id to mapping tables too
ALTER TABLE exercise_template_mappings ADD COLUMN program_id INTEGER REFERENCES programs(id);
ALTER TABLE routine_mappings ADD COLUMN program_id INTEGER REFERENCES programs(id);
```

**Scope of Changes**:

1. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/types.ts`** -- Add `program_id` to `QueueItemRow`, `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow`, `RoutineMappingRow`.

2. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/storage/queries.ts`** -- All queue and mapping queries need to scope by `program_id`:
   - `getQueueItems(db, userId)` becomes `getQueueItems(db, userId, programId)`.
   - `insertQueueItems` includes `program_id`.
   - Mapping queries scope by `program_id`.
   - New: `getPrograms(db, userId): Promise<ProgramRow[]>` -- list all programs for a user.
   - New: `setActiveProgram(db, userId, programId)` -- deactivate all, activate one.

3. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/program.ts`** -- New section: "Program Library"
   - List all uploaded programs with title, upload date, active status.
   - "Switch to this program" button.
   - "Delete program" button (with confirmation).

4. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts`** -- New routes:
   - `GET /programs` -- Program library page (or section on the Program tab).
   - `POST /api/switch-program/:id` -- Switch active program, load its queue state.
   - `DELETE /api/programs/:id` -- Delete a program and its queue items.

5. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/queue.ts`** -- `generatePlaylist` unchanged (pure function). The caller passes program-specific routines.

6. **`/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts`** -- `loadProgram` and all handlers that call it already work by loading the active program. The key change is that switching programs changes which program is active, and queue queries filter by `program_id`.

**Verification**:
- Upload two different programs. Verify both appear in the library.
- Switch between programs. Verify the Today page shows the correct queue for each.
- Verify queue progress is preserved when switching away and back.
- Verify deleting a program removes its queue items and mappings.

**Risk areas**:
- Hevy routine conflicts: two programs may create routines with the same name. The `reconcileRoutines` logic already handles this via folder-based dedup (each program gets its own folder).
- The `users.active_program` column stores a text label, not a FK. For multi-program support, this should reference `programs.id`. Decision: keep `active_program` as a display label for now, but add the actual FK lookup through `getActiveProgram` (which already queries by `is_active = 1`).
- Migration backfill assumes a single active program. For existing users this is correct.

---

## Worktree Setup

```
hevy-planner/           # main (stable)
hevy-planner-9/         # feat/data-driven-rendering
hevy-planner-7/         # feat/workout-details
hevy-planner-6/         # feat/webhooks
```

Wave 1 runs all three worktrees in parallel. After each completes and merges to `main`:

```
hevy-planner-4/         # feat/program-import (from updated main)
```

Then after #4 merges:

```
hevy-planner-5/         # feat/multi-program (from updated main)
```

## Execution Order Summary

| Wave | Issue | Branch | Depends On | Migration | Estimated Size |
|------|-------|--------|------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1a | #9 Data-Driven | `feat/data-driven-rendering` | None | None | Small (5 files, ~30 lines changed) |
| 1b | #7 Workout Details | `feat/workout-details` | None | 0005 | Medium (7 files, new domain fn) |
| 1c | #6 Webhooks | `feat/webhooks` | None | 0005 or 0006 | Medium (6 files, new routes) |
| 2 | #4 Program Import | `feat/program-import` | #9 merged | None | Medium (4 files, refactor setup) |
| 3 | #5 Multi-Program | `feat/multi-program` | #4 merged | 0006 or 0007 | Large (8+ files, schema change) |

## Migration Numbering Strategy

Since #7 and #6 run in parallel and both need migrations, pre-assign numbers:
- `0005_workout_data.sql` for #7
- `0006_webhook_state.sql` for #6
- `0007_multi_program.sql` for #5

This avoids numbering conflicts during parallel development.

## Per-Issue Verification Checklist

For every issue, before merge:
1. `npm run test` passes (all existing + new tests).
2. `wrangler dev` renders correctly.
3. `/simplify` pass: review for dead code, duplicated logic, or unnecessary complexity introduced.
4. Grep for hardcoded strings/magic values introduced by the change.
5. Review D1 queries for prepared statement usage (no string interpolation).

### Critical Files for Implementation
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/today.ts` - Hub of hardcoded values for #9; workout display surface for #7
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts` - Router and all handler logic; touched by every issue (#6 webhook route, #4 import route, #5 switch route, #7 pull handler, #9 handler cleanup)
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/storage/queries.ts` - Every D1 interaction; needs new columns (#7 workout data), new queries (#6 webhook state, #4 clear/import, #5 program scoping)
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/hevy/client.ts` - Webhook API methods for #6; workout data shapes for #7
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/reflow.ts` - Hardcoded "CARs only" string for #9; queue scoping changes for #5

toolUseResult.prompt:
Design an execution plan for the v2 milestone of hevy-planner, following Boris Cherny's Claude Code workflow principles: parallel worktrees, plan-then-1-shot, verification at every step, and /simplify after changes.

## v2 Issues (5 total)

### #9 ΓÇö Data-driven rendering
All static content rendered from program JSON, not hardcoded. Foundational ΓÇö blocks #4 and #5.
Current state: Nearly fully data-driven already. Remaining hardcoded items:
- Daily routine ID: routes/buttons use "daily" string literal (today.ts:19, index.ts route `/api/push-hevy/daily`)
- Label text: "Daily", "Next Session", "CARs only" (today.ts, reflow.ts:55)
- Folder names: "Daily" folder for isDaily routines (hevy-sync.ts:117)
- Daily routine color: hardcoded `var(--green)` (today.ts:22)
Key files: src/fragments/today.ts, src/domain/reflow.ts, src/domain/hevy-sync.ts, src/index.ts

### #7 ΓÇö Workout detail display (sets/reps/weights from Hevy)
Show actual workout data on completed queue items. Currently binary (done/not done).
Needs: Hevy workout data storage, display in fragments, comparison to prescribed sets.
Key files: src/hevy/client.ts, src/storage/queries.ts, src/fragments/today.ts, src/index.ts (handlePull)

### #6 ΓÇö Webhook-based auto-sync
Replace manual "Sync from Hevy" with webhooks. Hevy MCP tools include create-webhook-subscription, get-webhook-subscription, delete-webhook-subscription, get-workout-events.
Needs: webhook endpoint route, event processing, queue advancement.
Key files: src/index.ts, src/domain/hevy-sync.ts, src/hevy/client.ts

### #4 ΓÇö Program import/upload from JSON
Upload UI for program JSON, validate against schema, store in D1.
Currently: only bundled program loaded at setup. D1 `programs` table already stores JSON blobs.
Needs: upload UI fragment, validation endpoint, program storage, re-setup flow.
Key files: src/index.ts, src/fragments/, src/storage/queries.ts, schema/program.schema.json

### #5 ΓÇö Multiple program support
Program library, active switching, per-program queue state.
Currently: single active_program per user. Queue items reference routine_id (string from program JSON).
Needs: program list page, switching logic, queue scoped to program, parallel program option.
Key files: All of the above + new fragments, domain logic changes.

## Dependency Graph
```
#9 (data-driven) ΓöÇΓöÇΓåÆ #4 (import) ΓöÇΓöÇΓåÆ #5 (multi-program)
#7 (workout details) ΓÇö independent
#6 (webhooks) ΓÇö independent
```

## Current Architecture Context
- Cloudflare Workers + D1 + Datastar SSE
- Programs stored as JSON blobs in D1 `programs` table
- Queue: flat `queue_items` table with position-based ordering
- Hevy sync: push (setup) + pull (manual button), reconciliation via D1 mappings
- All domain logic pure functions in src/domain/
- Fragments return HTML strings with Datastar attributes
- 47 tests passing
- Content negotiation via Accept header (SSE vs HTML)

## Boris Method Requirements
1. **Parallel worktrees**: Identify which issues can be worked simultaneously
2. **Plan ΓåÆ 1-shot**: Each issue needs enough detail for a single implementation pass
3. **Verification**: Define how to test each change (tests, MCP tools, manual checks)
4. **/simplify**: Run after each issue to clean up

Design the execution plan with:
- Wave structure (what runs in parallel vs sequential)
- For each issue: scope, key changes, files modified, verification steps
- Migration needs (D1 schema changes)
- Risk areas and gotchas
